content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 8, 1994

Kit-Bacon Gressitt
Vice President
Community Youth Task Force
Towne Center, #229
P.O. Box 2511
Fallbrook, CA 92088

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-372

Dear Ms. Gressitt:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Bigs Parker, trustee with the Fallbrook Union Elementary
School District, regarding the appropriate use of campaign funds
pursuant to the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). I can confirmed
in our telephone conversation of December 6, 1994, Mr. Parker
consented to this advice request on his behalf.

Please note that this letter is based on the facts presented
to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in
providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May Bigs Parker, a trustee with the Fallbrook Union
Elementary School District, donate campaign funds to the Fallbrook
Chamber of Commerce (a civic organization formed pursuant to
Section 501(c)(4) of the Internal Revenue Code) to be used for a
community-wide youth symposium for strategic planning for youth
services in Fallbrook?

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-372
Page 2


CONCLUSION

It appears that the donation in question bears a reasonable
relationship to a governmental purpose. Thus, Mr. Parker may
donate campaign funds to the chamber of commerce so long as no
substantial part of the proceeds of the donation will have a
material financial effect on Mr. Parker or any member of his
immediate family.

FACTS

You are the Vice President of the Community Youth Task Force
(the "task force"). The task force is an unincorporated ad hoc
group of volunteers that assists at-risk youth in Fallbrook. The
task force is not operated for profit and maintains no bank
account. Donations are funneled directly through your group to
the program for which the donation is intended.

In the past, the task force created a boxing program for at-
risk youth, in collaboration with the local Boy's and Girl's club.
The task force is currently developing a community-wide youth
symposium for strategic planning for youth services in Fallbrook.

You stated that Bigs Parker, who was recently elected to the
Fallbrook Union Elementary School District, has offered to
contribute some of his campaign funds to the symposium project.
You plan to have the donation made to the Fallbrook Chamber of
Commerce, the sponsor of the project, to be held and used for the
youth symposium. You have asked whether Mr. Parker may make this
donation consistent with the provisions of the Act.

ANALYSIS

The general rule of the personal use provisions of the Act is
that an expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum,
reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose. (Section 89512; Section 89512.5.) Where an expenditure
of campaign funds confers a substantial personal benefit on the
candidate, the expenditure must be directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose.³


2
"Campaign funds" which may be donated include cash, cash
equivalents, and other assets of the committee. (Section
89511(b)(1).)

3
"Substantial personal benefit" means an expenditure of campaign
funds which results in a direct personal benefit with a value of
more than $100 to the candidate. (Section 89511(b)(3).)

Our File No. A-94-372
Page 3


However, the personal use statutes also provide specific
tests for certain types of expenditures. For example, campaign
funds may only be used to make donations to nonprofit
organizations if all of the following apply: (1) the recipient
organization is a bona fide charitable, educational, civic,
religious, or similar tax-exempt nonprofit organization; (2) no
substantial part of the proceeds of the donation will have a
material financial effect on the candidate or any member of his or
her immediate family, and (3) the donation bears a reasonable
relationship to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 89515.)

You stated that Mr. Parker wishes to donate his campaign
funds to the Fallbrook Chamber of Commerce (a civic organization
formed pursuant to Section 501(c)(4) of the Internal Revenue Code)
for use by your organization in developing a community-wide youth
symposium for strategic planning for youth services in Fallbrook.

In the Lolonis Advice Letter, No. A-94-250, we advised that
it was permissible under Section 89515 to donate campaign funds to
an civic organizations formed pursuant to Section 501(c)(4) of the
Internal Revenue Code, so long as no substantial part of the
proceeds of the donation will have a material financial effect on
the candidate or any member of his or her immediate family, and
the donation bears a reasonable relationship to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89515.)

It appears from your facts, that the donation bears a
reasonable relationship to a governmental purpose. Thus, so long
as no substantial part of the proceeds of the donation will have a
material financial effect on the candidate or any member of his or
her immediate family, the candidate may donate campaign funds to
the chamber of commerce.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

Thus, Mr. Parker may
